author: @McWhisperASMR
text: I created a male and female dovetail system and made it a creation. Can’t tell you how many things I’ve joined together with that system. Excellent stuff for those starting out. Tinkercad is truly the most user friendly 3d software and you can do some pretty impressive things if you think outside the box.

author: @baconsledge
text: I would recommend "Plasticity" for fast 3D print designs. Inexpensive but so fast to create the things you are using TinkerCad for. I have used Fusion, Alibre, Designad, etc., but Plasticity is amazing.

author: @pazguatos
text: I love how you can hear the birds outside, it fives a nice ambient to the video, very instructive for our future projects, thank you!

author: @stewartpalmer2456
text: As well, from me, thank you Dr. Smith. Different tools for different levels of learning. FreeCAD offers the polar array for this feature. For anyone wanting to seriously learn this stuff, learn to start with the flat sketch. A simple circle with the magnet holes as circles, then pad the thing. It's another learning curve opportunity. Never give up! Never surrender! After digesting Dr. Smith, check out Mango Jelly Solutions.

author: @Khwartz
text: Merci !

author: @DKFX1
text: Check this out Robert. If you duplicate and rotate the object (cylinder) and duplicate and rotate a few times, then you have a perfectly smooth surface which does not display the low res face counts when you print it. Save the high res cylinder in your "my creations tab" and you can readily use it as a cutting and shape template.

text:
Hi rob, anyone using solar power for electrolysis? I can confirm it works just hooking two bits of Copper up to the pos and neg and plonking them in some salt water. Waters like a blue lagoon! Only been going since about 10am.
Is that a viable.way to gain hydrogen out of water and also do some copper or brass or silver or gold plating. Gold might make more bubbles as its more conductive?

author: @VeniceInventors
text: It makes me wonder how tinkercad could possibly have such a bug? They must be doing something really silly with the internal representation of their primitives to end up losing precision during a rotation. I suspect that they're actually rotating each vertex instead of rotating the center of the object and then recalculating the vertices location.

author: @grendel1960a
text: I dont know tinkercad that well, but in other software i would create a circular array, I believe in tinkercad you make the first duplicate rotation, and then repeated presses of ctrl D repeats that operation for the next rotation, until you have finished, another way would be to take your first 2 holes and rotate these by double the angle, then take your 4 holes and duplicate and rotate 4x the angle, thus quickly reproducing all the holes required.

replies.author: @grendel1960a
replies.text:
@combitz is that even if you use the method outlined by Robert to lock the scale on the part? I am used to much more sophisticated CAD programs that dont behave like that, but appreciate that this is possible. Even in more complex CAD you do have to be very careful when choosing your axis of rotation or you will get wierd results. 
I wonder if it is the fractional dimensions that cause the issue or maybe if its a imperial/ metric approximation that is causing the main issue. it would be interesting to see if it is still an issue using imperial units. this is usually where imperfections can creep into software.
